items.q: How do greenhouse gases warm Earth?
items.correct: They absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate some back down.
items.misconception: They block heat from escaping into space (like a literal lid).
items.random_wrong: They reflect sunlight back to Earth.
items.why: Students picture a physical blanket. The mechanism is selective absorption - greenhouse gases let visible light pass IN but absorb outgoing infrared. The re-emission warms the lower atmosphere and surface.

items.q: Why is a cold winter not evidence against climate change?
items.correct: Climate refers to long-term averages, not individual weather events.
items.misconception: It is evidence - if it gets cold sometimes, warming must be wrong.
items.random_wrong: Cold winters are caused by warming.
items.why: Students conflate weather and climate. Climate is the LONG-TERM pattern (decades+); weather is short-term. Global warming refers to average trends - it allows individual cold events even as overall temperatures rise.

items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: The first step in the mitigation hierarchy is:
items.why: Correct answer: Avoid impact. “Offset impact” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 10% biodiversity net gain. “Carbon offsetting only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a project destroys 10 ha habitat and requires 10% net gain, the offset area (ha) must be at least?
items.why: The real figure is 11. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
